docs.title: SQL Database Utils
docs.text: A lightweight Python utility module for reading, writing, and manipulating data from SQL Server Databases. Optimized for use with pandas DataFrames.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• Read entire tables into pandas DataFrames
  • Filtered reads using dynamic WHERE clauses
  • Row deletion with WHERE conditions
  • Safe data insertion with parameterized queries (SQL injection protection)
  • Schema validation for DataFrame inserts
  • ODBC Driver 17 for SQL Server support

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
import sql_database_utils as db

# Connection string
conn_str = "DRIVER={ODBC Driver 17 for SQL Server};SERVER=server;DATABASE=db;UID=user;PWD=password"

# Read an entire table
df = db.read_table("Customers", conn_str)

# Read with filter
df_filtered = db.read_with_filter("Orders", conn_str, "OrderAmount", "> 500")

# Insert data
db.insert_dataframe("NewTable", df, conn_str)

# Delete rows
deleted_count = db.delete_with_filter("Orders", conn_str, "OrderStatus", "= 'Cancelled'")

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
• Installation - Get started with the module
  • Usage Guide - Detailed usage examples
  • API Reference - Complete API documentation
  • Building & Deployment - Build and package information

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Python 3.10+
  • pyodbc
  • pandas
  • ODBC Driver 17 for SQL Server

docs.title: Building the Package
docs.text: The module is packaged using setuptools. To build a wheel distribution:

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Ensure you have the build tools installed:

pip install build wheel setuptools

docs.title: Build Commands
docs.text:
Clean previous builds:

python setup.py clean --all

Build the wheel:

python setup.py bdist_wheel

The built wheel will be located in the dist/ folder.

docs.title: Output
docs.text:
dist/
├── sql_database_utils-0.2.0-py3-none-any.whl
└── sql_database_utils-0.2.0.tar.gz

docs.title: Installation from Built Wheel
docs.text:
Once the wheel is built, you can install it locally:

pip install dist/sql_database_utils-0.2.0-py3-none-any.whl

docs.title: Uploading to PyPI
docs.text:
To upload the package to PyPI:

  1. Install twine: 

    pip install twine

  2. Upload to PyPI: 

    twine upload dist/*

  3. Or upload to TestPyPI first: 

    twine upload --repository testpypi dist/*

docs.title: GitHub Releases
docs.text:
Attach the wheel and source distributions to GitHub releases for distribution:

# The wheel and tarball in dist/ can be attached to releases

docs.title: Install in Development Mode
docs.text:
# Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/yxuan1996/sql_server_database_utils.git
cd sql_server_database_utils

# Install dependencies
pip install -r requirements.txt

# Install module in editable mode
pip install -e .

docs.title: Install Documentation Tools
docs.text:
To build and serve documentation locally:

pip install mkdocs mkdocs-material pymdown-extensions

docs.title: Build Documentation
docs.text:
# Build static site
mkdocs build

# Serve locally for development
mkdocs serve

The documentation will be available at http://localhost:8000

docs.title: Version Management
docs.text:
The current version is 0.2.0 as defined in setup.py. To update the version:

  1. Update version in setup.py
  2. Update version references in documentation if needed
  3. Create a new git tag
git tag -a v0.2.0 -m "Release version 0.2.0"
git push origin v0.2.0

docs.text:
• pyodbc - Python ODBC interface

For setting up CI/CD pipelines, consider:

docs.title: Issue: ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'setuptools'
docs.text:
Solution: 

pip install setuptools wheel

docs.title: Issue: python setup.py bdist_wheel command not found
docs.text:
Solution: 

python -m build

docs.title: Issue: Wheel builds but fails to install
docs.text: Ensure all dependencies are properly listed in requirements.txt and specified in setup.py.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before installing sql_database_utils, ensure you have the following installed:

  • Python 3.10 or higher
  • ODBC Driver 17 for SQL Server - Required for database connectivity

docs.title: On Windows
docs.text: Download and install from the Microsoft ODBC Driver for SQL Server.

docs.title: On macOS
docs.text:
# Using Homebrew
brew tap microsoft/mssql-release https://github.com/Microsoft/homebrew-mssql-release
brew install msodbcsql17 mssql-tools

docs.title: On Linux (Ubuntu/Debian)
docs.text:
curl https://packages.microsoft.com/keys/microsoft.asc | apt-key add -
curl https://packages.microsoft.com/config/ubuntu/$(lsb_release -rs)/prod.list > /etc/apt/sources.list.d/mssql-release.list
apt-get update
apt-get install -y msodbcsql17

docs.text: pip install sql-database-utils

docs.title: From Source
docs.text:
1. Clone the repository: 

    git clone https://github.com/yxuan1996/sql_server_database_utils.git
cd sql_server_database_utils

  2. Install dependencies: 

    pip install -r requirements.txt

  3. Install the module: 

    pip install -e .

docs.title: Verifying Installation
docs.text:
To verify the installation was successful, try importing the module:

import sql_database_utils

print(dir(sql_database_utils))

You should see the available functions listed.

docs.title: Connection String Setup
docs.text: To use sql_database_utils, you'll need a valid ODBC connection string to your SQL Server database.

docs.title: Connection String Format
docs.text: DRIVER={ODBC Driver 17 for SQL Server};SERVER=server_name;DATABASE=database_name;UID=username;PWD=password

docs.title: Example Connection Strings
docs.text:
Local SQL Server: 

conn_str = "DRIVER={ODBC Driver 17 for SQL Server};SERVER=localhost;DATABASE=MyDatabase;UID=sa;PWD=password"

Azure SQL Database: 

conn_str = "DRIVER={ODBC Driver 17 for SQL Server};SERVER=myserver.database.windows.net;DATABASE=MyDatabase;UID=user@myserver;PWD=password"

Using Windows Authentication: 

conn_str = "DRIVER={ODBC Driver 17 for SQL Server};SERVER=server_name;DATABASE=database_name;Trusted_Connection=yes"

docs.title: Read Entire Table
docs.text:
Use read_table() to read an entire table into a pandas DataFrame:

import sql_database_utils as db

conn_str = "DRIVER={ODBC Driver 17 for SQL Server};SERVER=localhost;DATABASE=MyDB;UID=user;PWD=password"

# Read entire table
df = db.read_table("Customers", conn_str)
print(df.head())

docs.title: Read with Filter
docs.text:
Use read_with_filter() to read only rows that match a condition:

# Read orders with amount > 500
df_large_orders = db.read_with_filter(
    "Orders", 
    conn_str, 
    "OrderAmount", 
    "> 500"
)

# Read specific customer
df_customer = db.read_with_filter(
    "Customers", 
    conn_str, 
    "CustomerID", 
    "= 123"
)

# Read by date range
df_recent = db.read_with_filter(
    "TransactionLog", 
    conn_str, 
    "TransactionDate", 
    ">= '2024-01-01'"
)

docs.title: Insert DataFrame
docs.text:
Use insert_dataframe() to insert data from a pandas DataFrame into a SQL Server table:

import pandas as pd

# Create a sample DataFrame
df = pd.DataFrame({
    'CustomerID': [1, 2, 3],
    'Name': ['Alice', 'Bob', 'Charlie'],
    'Email': ['alice@example.com', 'bob@example.com', 'charlie@example.com']
})

# Insert into table
db.insert_dataframe("Customers", df, conn_str)

The function will:

  • Retrieve the table schema from the database
  • Validate columns - ensures no extra columns exist in the DataFrame
  • Auto-fill missing columns with NULL values
  • Use parameterized queries to prevent SQL injection
  • Insert row-by-row for safe operations

Note

For large batch inserts (>10,000 rows), consider breaking the DataFrame into smaller chunks for better performance.

docs.title: Deleting Data
docs.text:
Use delete_with_filter() to delete rows matching a condition:

# Delete cancelled orders
deleted_count = db.delete_with_filter(
    "Orders",
    conn_str,
    "OrderStatus",
    "= 'Cancelled'"
)

print(f"{deleted_count} rows deleted")

# Delete old log entries
deleted_count = db.delete_with_filter(
    "TransactionLog",
    conn_str,
    "TransactionDate",
    "< '2020-01-01'"
)

print(f"Deleted {deleted_count} old transactions")

docs.title: Security
docs.text:
Warning

Always use parameterized queries and avoid string concatenation for user input. The module's functions are designed to use parameterized queries internally.

docs.title: Performance
docs.text:
• Reading: For large tables, use filtering to reduce memory usage
  • Inserting: Insert dataframes in batches of 1,000-5,000 rows for optimal speed
  • Deleting: Test your WHERE conditions carefully before executing

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
import sql_database_utils as db

try:
    df = db.read_table("NonExistentTable", conn_str)
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error reading table: {e}")

try:
    db.insert_dataframe("Customers", df, conn_str)
except Exception as e:
    print(f"Error inserting data: {e}")

docs.title: Data Pipeline Example
docs.text:
import sql_database_utils as db
import pandas as pd

conn_str = "DRIVER={ODBC Driver 17 for SQL Server};SERVER=localhost;DATABASE=MyDB;UID=user;PWD=password"

# 1. Read raw data
raw_df = db.read_table("RawData", conn_str)

# 2. Process data
processed_df = raw_df[raw_df['Amount'] > 0].copy()
processed_df['Date'] = pd.to_datetime(processed_df['Date'])

# 3. Store processed data
db.insert_dataframe("ProcessedData", processed_df, conn_str)

# 4. Clean up old entries
deleted = db.delete_with_filter("RawData", conn_str, "Status", "= 'Processed'")
print(f"Cleaned up {deleted} records")

docs.title: Bulk Data Migration
docs.text:
# Read from source table
df = db.read_with_filter(
    "SourceTable",
    conn_str_source,
    "Year",
    ">= 2023"
)

# Insert to destination table
db.insert_dataframe("DestinationTable", df, conn_str_destination)

print(f"Migrated {len(df)} records")

docs.title: read_table
docs.text:
Reads an entire table from SQL Server into a pandas DataFrame.

def read_table(table_name: str, conn_str: str) -> pd.DataFrame

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Description table_name str Name of the table to read conn_str str ODBC connection string

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: • pd.DataFrame - DataFrame containing the table data

docs.title: Raises
docs.text: • Exception - If table doesn't exist or connection fails

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
import sql_database_utils as db

conn_str = "DRIVER={ODBC Driver 17 for SQL Server};SERVER=localhost;DATABASE=MyDB;UID=user;PWD=password"
df = db.read_table("Customers", conn_str)
print(df.shape)
print(df.head())

docs.title: read_with_filter
docs.text:
Reads a filtered set of rows from a table using a WHERE clause.

def read_with_filter(table_name: str, conn_str: str, column_name: str, condition: str) -> pd.DataFrame

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Description table_name str Name of the table to read conn_str str ODBC connection string column_name str Column name for the filter condition str SQL condition (e.g., "= 'value'", "> 10")

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: • pd.DataFrame - DataFrame containing filtered rows

docs.title: Raises
docs.text: • Exception - If column doesn't exist or query fails

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
import sql_database_utils as db

conn_str = "DRIVER={ODBC Driver 17 for SQL Server};SERVER=localhost;DATABASE=MyDB;UID=user;PWD=password"

# Numeric comparison
df = db.read_with_filter("Orders", conn_str, "OrderAmount", "> 500")

# String matching
df = db.read_with_filter("Employees", conn_str, "Country", "= 'USA'")

# Date comparison
df = db.read_with_filter("Transactions", conn_str, "TransactionDate", ">= '2024-01-01'")

docs.title: delete_with_filter
docs.text:
Deletes rows from a SQL Server table based on a WHERE condition.

def delete_with_filter(table_name: str, conn_str: str, column_name: str, condition: str) -> int

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Description table_name str Name of the table to delete from conn_str str ODBC connection string column_name str Column name for the filter condition str SQL condition (e.g., "= 'value'", "> 10")

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: • int - Number of rows deleted

docs.title: Raises
docs.text: • Exception - If table doesn't exist or query fails

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
import sql_database_utils as db

conn_str = "DRIVER={ODBC Driver 17 for SQL Server};SERVER=localhost;DATABASE=MyDB;UID=user;PWD=password"

# Delete cancelled orders
deleted = db.delete_with_filter("Orders", conn_str, "Status", "= 'Cancelled'")
print(f"Deleted {deleted} orders")

# Delete old records
deleted = db.delete_with_filter("AuditLog", conn_str, "CreatedDate", "< '2020-01-01'")
print(f"Deleted {deleted} old audit records")

docs.title: insert_dataframe
docs.text:
Inserts data from a pandas DataFrame into a SQL Server table.

def insert_dataframe(table_name: str, dataframe: pd.DataFrame, conn_str: str) -> None

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Description table_name str Name of the table to insert into dataframe pd.DataFrame DataFrame containing data to insert conn_str str ODBC connection string

docs.title: Process
docs.text:
1. Schema Retrieval - Fetches the table schema from the database
  2. Validation - Checks for extra columns in the DataFrame
  3. Missing Columns - Auto-fills missing columns with NULL
  4. Parameterized Insert - Generates safe INSERT queries
  5. Row-by-Row Insert - Inserts one row at a time

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: • None

docs.title: Raises
docs.text: • Exception - If table doesn't exist, has extra columns, or insert fails

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
import pandas as pd
import sql_database_utils as db

conn_str = "DRIVER={ODBC Driver 17 for SQL Server};SERVER=localhost;DATABASE=MyDB;UID=user;PWD=password"

# Create DataFrame
df = pd.DataFrame({
    'CustomerID': [1, 2, 3],
    'Name': ['Alice', 'Bob', 'Charlie'],
    'Email': ['alice@example.com', 'bob@example.com', 'charlie@example.com']
})

# Insert data
db.insert_dataframe("Customers", df, conn_str)
print("Data inserted successfully")

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The DataFrame columns must match the table's columns
  • Missing columns in the DataFrame will be set to NULL
  • Extra columns will cause an error
  • Parameterized queries prevent SQL injection
  • Row-by-row insertion is safe but slower; consider batching for large datasets

docs.title: Format
docs.text: DRIVER={ODBC Driver 17 for SQL Server};SERVER=server;DATABASE=database;UID=user;PWD=password

docs.title: Common Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Description Example DRIVER ODBC driver version {ODBC Driver 17 for SQL Server} SERVER Server address localhost, server.database.windows.net DATABASE Database name MyDatabase UID Username user@domain PWD Password SecurePassword123 Trusted_Connection Use Windows auth yes
